title: Task 2: Add derived structural metadata during normalization

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: Task 2 sounds simple on paper: Preserve more useful signal about tool-heavy trajectories without redesigning the schema.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: And the reason it matters is straightforward: Phase 0 established that the corpus is tool-dense and structure-heavy. The current normalized format is appropriately simple, but it throws away cheap-to-compute metadata that would help diagnostics and filtering.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: What I like here is that the implementation does not chase a grand redesign. It tightens one seam in the pipeline.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. The implemented result for Task 2 is now marked done in the plan, with concrete verification commands recorded for the repo.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [gasp] So what changes downstream because Task 2 is already implemented?

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: This metadata becomes the cheap signal that later filtering, diagnostics, and evaluation slices can depend on.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: That means the value is not just local correctness. It is leverage for everything that comes after.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: With richer metadata in place, anomaly taxonomy and perturbation analysis stop being blind to trajectory structure.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [chuckle] This is very home-lab coded: small patch, big increase in trust.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Yes. It follows the same discipline across the whole plan: keep the architecture modest, but make the data and labels harder to fool.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: And because Task 2 is complete, the listener can treat this as a concrete repo change rather than a hypothetical roadmap item.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. This episode is not selling a future intention. It is documenting an implemented improvement and the next layer of value it unlocks.
